query: evidence on minimally invasive vs traditional TMJ surgery effectiveness

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Facial Muscle Energy, Direct MFR, and BLT Procedure – for TMJ Dysfunction -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Muscle energy uses the patient's muscle energy to control a pathological barrier via reciprocal and autogenic inhibition. Administered by manual therapists, including physical therapists, physiotherapists, and non-physician osteopaths, this method has undergone randomized control trials to prove its efficacy. Phadke et al analyzed the effect of muscle energy procedures and static stretching on pain management and functional disability in patients with mechanical neck pain, similar to symptoms seen in TMJ dysfunctions. The team concluded that MET was better than the static stretching techniques used by professionals, judging outcomes using VAS and the Neck Disability Index (NDI). [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Facial Muscle Energy, Direct MFR, and BLT Procedure – for TMJ Dysfunction -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Despite an official merger between osteopathic (DO) and allopathic (MD) graduate medical education, no steps have been taken to integrate OMT practice as a rotation across ACGME-accredited residency programs. DOs and MDs both learn Western, allopathic medicine, and surgery to graduate medical school. Yet, MDs have traditionally avoided practicing OMT, the only skill differentiating an MD from a DO. Understanding and implementing the side-effect-free analgesic benefit of OMT would allow for a broader approach to clinical practice and offer patients various treatment options.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Facial Muscle Energy, Direct MFR, and BLT Procedure – for TMJ Dysfunction -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify TMJ as a frequent cause of headaches and pain with mastication. Assess key anatomical landmarks associated with the TMJ and the role they play in osteopathic treatment. Evaluate the steps needed to perform a physical exam specific to the TMJ. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Facial Muscle Energy, Direct MFR, and BLT Procedure – for TMJ Dysfunction -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: OMT-led injuries are rare in the literature; a retrospective study of cases administered between 1925 and 1993 reports only 185 injuries caused by OMT. Acute soreness is sometimes apparent in patients after OMT; any form of manual medicine leads to this soreness. The degree of soreness varies, as it solely depends on the approach, angle, and force the physician or non-physician therapist applies. Passive interventions like BLT procedures are generally risk-free; however, active interventions such as muscle energy may cause minor musculoskeletal complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Facial Muscle Energy, Direct MFR, and BLT Procedure – for TMJ Dysfunction -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The temporomandibular joint is pivotal to mastication and vocalization. Dysfunction in this joint can occur with microtrauma or maladaptive behaviors. A myriad of treatment options exist, both invasive and noninvasive. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of temporomandibular dysfunction. It highlights the osteopathic provider's role in evaluating and treating this condition utilizing muscle energy, myofascial release, and balanced ligamentous technique.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Facial Muscle Energy, Direct MFR, and BLT Procedure – for TMJ Dysfunction -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Western surgical and medicinal interventions are effective in pain management for TMJ-related disorders; however, both DOs and MDs undermine OMT's pain-free application in clinical practice and pain management relating to TMJ dysfunctions. There are a few significant reasons why DOs (osteopathic physicians) less-frequently practice OMT after medical school graduation and residency completion: Lack of conjoining between osteopathic principles and clinical concepts in the medical and educational process. Extended periods of graduate medical training that fissions students from the traditional classroom-based OMT experience. [13]

retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasound presents potential advantages over traditional methods for measuring condylar mobility and TMD (tongue thickness, TT) for airway assessment. The undiagnosed cases of temporomandibular disorders during surgery are mentioned, highlighting the limitations of the maximal mouth-opening method for assessing TMJ mobility.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Facial Muscle Energy, Direct MFR, and BLT Procedure – for TMJ Dysfunction -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Equipment necessary for OMT techniques to treat TMJ dysfunction include: An exam table An OMT table A massage table An alternative location that comforts the patient in sitting allows the physician to perform the technique comfortably.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Facial Muscle Energy, Direct MFR, and BLT Procedure – for TMJ Dysfunction -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Even though popularly used, facial muscle energy is an active and direct intervention to the pathological barrier. The muscle energy technique requires the physician to employ some emphatic forces. The physician must take the responsibility to reconsider this intervention if the patient has the following: Low vitality Jaw fractures Unstable joints Acute surgical record MFR, although passive, can be a direct or indirect intervention. Therefore, MFR is unsuitable for patients of all ages and conditions. The physician must reconsider treatment if the patient has: Healing fractures Advanced diabetes Severe osteoporosis Rheumatoid arthritis Malignancy Aneurysm BLT is a passive, direct/ indirect intervention. It is much more tolerable compared to other active alternatives. However, some patients may not be suitable for the procedure if the patient: Refuses consent Has acute jaw fractures Shows temporal bone malignancies Has a history of osteomyelitis [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Facial Muscle Energy, Direct MFR, and BLT Procedure – for TMJ Dysfunction -- Technique or Treatment -- Facial MET for the TMJ
retrieved_docs.content: With the patient in a supine position: Sit at the head of the table and instruct the patient to slowly open the mouth to observe exaggerated deviation to the side of the restriction; Slowly translate the mandible in neutral, flexion, and extension to identify a TMJ restriction; If dizziness or nausea appears, discontinue the procedure; With support from your hand opposite to the barrier, mobilize the lateral mandible into its flexion-extension along with restrictive barriers using the right index and middle fingers; instruct the patient to slowly open their mouth from the restriction against your equal resistance for 3 to 5 seconds; The patient should relax and then push the mandible to a new side-bending restrictive barrier; Repeat this counteractive contraction and stretch 3 to 5 times or until the barrier is engaged; Reassess TMJ's motion to confirm the engagement.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 85% to 90% of TMJ disorder symptoms are managed with noninvasive, nonsurgical approaches. Noninvasive management strategies include manual physiotherapy and osteopathy, patient education, pharmacological treatment, and splint therapy. [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Invasive Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Invasive strategies include intra-articular long-acting corticosteroid, hyaluronic acid, and botox injections. These interventions are recommended once conservative therapies have failed or in severe acute exacerbations.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Facial Muscle Energy, Direct MFR, and BLT Procedure – for TMJ Dysfunction -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dentists have reported TMJ dysfunction in dental literature since the 1950s, initially termed temporomandibular dysfunction; it involved malocclusion of the teeth (imbalance between the upper and lower teeth). Experts later discovered that this mechanism alone was insufficient to account for all clinical findings in the condition. About 20 years later, in 1979, it was conceptually designated as an orthopedic condition. TMJ disorders are still coined temporomandibular disorders (TMD), containing all TMJ problems and related structures. OMT (osteopathic manipulative therapy) mobilizes the compromised TMJ through the dysfunctional pathologic barrier (not after its usual physiological barrier), leading to low injury reports of OMT procedures. This review explores the usage of OMT in treating TMD. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Facial Muscle Energy, Direct MFR, and BLT Procedure – for TMJ Dysfunction -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Muscle energy procedure is the most risk-bearing in this band of procedures, requiring the physician to apply direct exertion. Medical literature reports 1 deleterious result per 50,000 active interventions; however, no strong evidence suggests that active manual interventions performed by well-trained physicians (DO or MD) and providers should go through revision due to high risk. Proper warm-up before and adequate hydration after OMT procedures minimize soreness; experience enhances the physician's or provider's skills and leads to better performance, as well. [9] [10] [11]

Specific arthroscopic techniques, such as removal of a loose body from a joint, illustrate minimally invasive applications of the procedure. Arthrotomy with prosthesis removal and incision‑and‑excision of joint structures are also categorized as “other or therapeutic procedures on joints,” employing a joint incision (بضع المفصل) as the operative method. Joint‑related pathology is linked to the Arabic term اعتلال مفصلي (joint disease) and is grouped under “other non‑traumatic joint disorders,” connecting metabolic or endocrine arthropathies to the general spectrum of non‑traumatic joint disease. Incision procedures (Incision–action) serve as methodological approaches across multiple therapeutic domains. retrieved_docs.content: The term “Other OR therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system” functions as a classification label for the broader category “other or therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system.” Within this classified group, the specific intervention “Reopening of osteotomy site of facial bone” is regarded as a related and possibly synonymous procedure, indicating that it belongs to the same set of musculoskeletal therapeutic actions.

It is identified as the “method_of” other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, suggesting that excisional techniques are employed both in therapeutic and aesthetic interventions of the nasal area. Overall, the knowledge base positions Excision as a central operative modality encompassing a wide range of minimally invasive and open surgical techniques across orthopedics, general surgery, neurosurgery, and endocrine surgery, while simultaneously defining its boundaries by excluding specific laminectomy approaches and connecting it to both therapeutic and plastic procedures of the facial region.

The “Incision – action” procedural method is shared across diverse interventions, including placement of a suprapubic catheter (تركيب قسطرة فوق العانة), corneal scraping (رأب القرنية), nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy, and reopening of an osteotomy site of a facial bone. Arthroscopic procedures are captured by the entity “Arthroscopic procedure of joint, not elsewhere classified,” which maps to “Other operations on joint structures” and represents the broader concept of التنظير المفصلي (arthroscopy). A specific example, “Arthroscopic removal of loose body from joint,” illustrates that arthroscopy is a subset of joint‑focused therapeutic actions. Arthroscopy is defined as a non‑arthroplastic joint operation—i.e., a joint procedure that does not replace the joint surface—and is positioned as a child of orthopedic corrective surgery (عملية جراحة تقويم العظام). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2056
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical Manipulation is a broad category of operative interventions that includes specific techniques such as Application of Therapeutic Agent and Manual Conversion of Position. Application of Therapeutic Agent refers to the intra‑operative delivery of medication or other therapeutic substances, while Manual Conversion of Position involves the physical repositioning of tissues, organs, or skeletal structures during a procedure. Both Application of Therapeutic Agent and Manual Conversion of Position are considered child concepts within Surgical Manipulation, illustrating the diverse range of actions that fall under this overarching surgical domain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2833
